nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; reported over sand and coral bottoms of clear channels and reef flats at shallow depths (0–6 m). Possibly endemic to Kapingamarangi Atoll in the Caroline Islands.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on benthic algae in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer marine-based seaweed (nori) on a clip at least three times per week, supplemented with spirulina-based pellets/flakes and occasional meaty foods such as mysis or brine shrimp. A vegetable-heavy diet is commonly considered important for supporting immune function, coloration, and reduced aggression.

summary: A rare Western Pacific surgeonfish, possibly endemic to Kapingamarangi Atoll. Generally considered reef-safe and a useful algae grazer; tends to be aggressive toward other tangs and similarly shaped fishes.
careNotes:
The Mimic Half Black Tang (Acanthurus chronixis) is a rarely-encountered surgeonfish that, in the wild, is possibly endemic to Kapingamarangi Atoll in the Caroline Islands (Western Pacific). FishBase notes that reports of this species from other regions are likely misidentifications of juvenile Acanthurus pyroferus, which mimics the Lemonpeel Angelfish — meaning many fish sold in the trade as 'A. chronixis' may not be the true species. Adults have been described as dark brown with a darker band at the base of the dorsal fin and a distinctive contrasting half-black caudal/posterior region; juveniles are reported to display lighter tan coloration with black trim.

Like all Acanthurus surgeonfish, this species carries a sharp, blade-like caudal-peduncle spine (scalpel) that can inflict deep cuts on handlers and tankmates — caution is generally advised when netting or transferring. Tangs are also commonly considered prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or fed an algae-poor diet, so a varied vegetable-heavy diet and stable water quality are commonly considered important.

This species has been known to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other surgeonfish, but tends to be peaceful with unrelated fish. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen per aquarium unless the system is very large. A well-established reef tank with ample live rock for grazing, strong water movement, and open swimming space is generally preferred.

Source disagreement notes: Minimum tank volume varies — LiveAquaria states 125 gal (~473 L) and Quality Marine states 180 gal (~681 L); the value used (700 L) errs to the larger, cautious side given the species' size and active-swimmer profile. Adult size varies across sources (LiveAquaria 10 in/25 cm; Quality Marine 11 in/28 cm; FishBase max 28 cm TL). Care level varies (LiveAquaria 'Easy'; Quality Marine 'Moderate'); intermediate selected as the average and reflecting general tang husbandry norms. Lifespan in captivity is poorly documented; 5–10 years is a hobbyist estimate consistent with similar Acanthurus species.
